ground_truth.title: What to look for before purchasing new fish
ground_truth.author: AquaCare
ground_truth.main_content:
AquaCare
Aug 08, 2006

I get asked quite often, especially by beginning aquarists, what it is they should be looking for, or at, when purchasing a new fish. I was thinking that now was as good a time as any to put together a list of suggestions. I'll get us started here, if anybody has some that they'd like to add, please feel free to do so. I'm not going to try and put these in any particular order, but I'm sure that's something we could do once we get going.

1) Look at the general condition of all the animals in the tank. Granted, any LFS is a stressful place for a fish even under the best conditions, but they should have decent color, free of patchy areas. They should look well fed, but not bloated. If you have any doubts, or you're working with a notoriously fussy species, ask to see them eat. Also check how they're swimming. The fish should be able to move about freely without struggling.

2) Check for the common communicable diseases and parasites such as ich, velvet, fin and tail rot, parasitic worms, pop eye/eye cloud, and any blood streaks in the fins. If you see any fish in the tank with any of these problems, then ALL the fish in the tank are suspect as well and none of them are safe for purchase. If the tank in question is part of an integrated system and not isolated, then the fish in all of the tanks in that loop are also suspect and the fish are not safe for purchase. For those who might not know, an integrated system is one in which all the tanks are connected to one central pump so therefore share the water between them and also any pathogens or parasites. If you're not sure which system it is and you spot a sick animal, be sure to ask before buying fish out of any of the tanks.

3) Ask what pH and temperature the fish are being kept at. While this may not be critical for some species, for other it is. Cardinals, Rummynose, Rams, Discus, Clown loaches, and Bleeding heart tetras are just a few examples of fish that can be sensitive to both pH and changes in pH. If they don't know what these basic parameters are, ask them to test it for you. If they can't test it, seriously question the quality of any fish there.

4) Make sure that the species your considering purchasing is compatible with you and with its prospective tankmates (if any). The best way to accomplish this is to do your homework before you purchase that new species. Unless you know the person selling the fish pretty well and are completely confident in their knowledge, don't take their word for it, do some research. Come to think of it, do your research no matter what. With the internet around, it's fun, it's easy, and as much as I hate to admit it, all of us can make a mistake once in awhile.

5) Watch the person catching the fish. Any competent LFS personnel should be able to catch any given fish in the store without having to run it down to the point of exhaustion. If they do, tell them you no longer want that fish. The upcoming transfer to their new home and environment is stressful enough, you don't want to start out with a fish that's already stressed by exhaustion.

Well there's a start. I'm sure there's probably some more out there, but this gets us going.

Marn
Sep 01, 2006

listen to your kids .. when your 8 yr old walks up to the tanks and says oooooo look at the sparkly fish ... leave it alone .. my son did that in Walmart and they all had Ick and a really bad case of it ..

Flameblade
Apr 04, 2009

lol, that would be sad if somebody added those to an established tank thinking "ooh, a sparkly fish!"

discusfishies
Mar 22, 2011

Make sure there are no dead fish in the tank :)

Diana
Mar 23, 2011

Have a quarantine tank set up or ready to set up. Never add new fish to your main tank right away. They may have a low grade infection or infestation that does not show yet. Give them a month in quarantine to be sure they are clean of most potential diseases and parasites.

Develop a friendship with at least one of the employees in the store. Go in when that person is on duty. This sort of friendship has saved me from taking home several diseased fish. One time my friend warned me that certain fish from a particular supplier should be watched before purchase. Sure enough, within a few days the fish (still at the store) started dying of Neon Tetra Disease.

aquatic hoyt
Jul 08, 2013

with fish make sure they have been in store for awhile to insure if they get any disease they wont get it anymore unless its winter and you run your bags of fish inside and with the water suddenly being cold it produces ich !

Firemouth
Jul 08, 2013

make sure the person who helps u doesnt pick the weakest one so he or she can get u out of their way, tell them wat u want

lou zello
Jul 08, 2013

everything that was said to open this subject was perfect!!!!! I would like to add this::ask the sales person to " DOUBLEBAG" your fish in this manner:::: after getting fish/inverts etc. in first bag,open second bag and insert first bag upside down into second(tied/rubber band end is in bottom of second bag) no need to tie end of second bag=====RESULT== NO CORNERS FOR FISH/INVERTS ETC. to get caught in on the way home!!!!! RESULT== NO DEAD/DROWNED FISH/INVERTS ETC.!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

C. Andrew Nelson
Jul 11, 2013

Good point! My LFS puts all their fish through more than a month of quarantine before putting them out in their main tanks for sale. I truly appreciate that.

C. Andrew Nelson
Jul 11, 2013

The best advice I can give is to know the store you're purchasing from. Really get to know them and their practices. How do they approach the business? Is it simply a money-making venture for them or are they hobbyists as well? If it is a chain store like Petsmart or Petco then visit that particular location several times over the course of a month or two to truly get a feel for how well their tanks are maintained, how experienced their employees are, and how health their stock is. If possible, find out how often they get new stock in, what they are feeding the fish, etc.

And the hardest part of all...don't buy fish on a whim.

Schmidtsie
Jul 14, 2013

You are going to have to make a dedicated post just about your trip to Spencer Jack's instead of mentioning it in every other post.

Crazygar
Jul 14, 2013

What I do is have them tape the corners to the side of the bag, ensuring to crush points where Fish can get trapped or have them double bag it with the round part (top) of the first bag at the bottom of the second bag.

Crazygar
Jul 14, 2013

Hmmmm I might just reference it more thanks bud.

aquatic hoyt
Aug 08, 2013

agreed. the nice thing is though the owner of my lfs is more my friend and pays me a visit to look at the tanks I own we have a barter system going on I make baby plants he gives me plants I want! simple and I'm happy with it!

aquatic hoyt
Aug 08, 2013

plus this guy bred 800 dollar stingrays in his store tank! quiet a profit I was thinking but he just wanted to see the birth!
